summary: An interview excerpt detailing Walt Disney's collaborative leadership style. It highlights his preference for cross-functional brainstorming, his reluctance to give direct compliments to keep his team grounded, and his strict rule against criticizing ideas without offering constructive improvements.

principles.statement: Never criticize an idea without offering a way to improve it.
principles.rationale: He wanted positive thinkers who focused on 'plussing' ideas—taking a good idea and making it great—rather than just tearing things down.
principles.source_quote: the one thing he hated was if somebody didn't like something and didn't give any comment about how to make it better how to improve it

principles.statement: Avoid excessive praise to keep the team hungry and focused.
principles.rationale: He believed that giving direct compliments would spoil people and make them too cocky.
principles.source_quote: he didn't ever want to spoil you and make you feel too cocky so all he'd ever say was that'll work

principles.statement: Gather diverse disciplines in one room to generate the best ideas.
principles.rationale: He loved working in teams and wanted everyone, regardless of their specific role, to contribute their unique perspectives to the project.
principles.source_quote: an entire group of people get together story men and animators and songwriters and art background people and script writers all bet together in one room and talk about the [Music] project and he like to have everyone contribute their ideas

mental_models.name: The Pollinating Bee
mental_models.description: A leadership model where the leader moves between different teams and disciplines, collecting small insights ('honey') from each and spreading them around to cross-pollinate ideas across the organization.
mental_models.example: Walt moving between story men, animators, and songwriters, taking ideas from one group and sharing them with others to elevate the entire project.

heuristics: If you don't like something, you must suggest how to make it better.
Plus it: take a good idea and make it a great idea.

signature_quotes.context: Walt's highest form of a compliment, used to validate an idea without making the creator arrogant.

anti_patterns.description: Criticizing an idea without offering a solution or improvement.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It lacks positive thinking and fails to contribute to 'plussing' the project.

anti_patterns.description: Giving direct, effusive compliments to team members.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It spoils people and makes them feel too cocky, reducing their drive to improve.
